title: Site Selection
description: Pomegranate thrives in hot, dry tropical and subtropical climates. Choose sites with full sun exposure (at least 6 hours daily) and good air circulation to minimise fungal disease pressure.
tips: Optimal temperature range: 25–38°C during growing season
Can tolerate light frost but young plants need protection below 5°C
Altitude up to 1,800 m above sea level is suitable
Avoid frost pockets and low-lying areas prone to waterlogging

title: Soil Preparation
description: Pomegranate is highly adaptable but performs best in well-drained, loamy soil with a slightly acidic to neutral pH. Heavy clay and waterlogged soils must be avoided.
tips: Ideal pH range: 5.5–7.0
Prepare pits 60 cm × 60 cm × 60 cm at least 30 days before planting
Mix excavated soil with 20 kg FYM (Farm Yard Manure), 1 kg Single Super Phosphate, 0.5 kg Muriate of Potash per pit
Apply Trichoderma viride 50 g per pit to suppress soil-borne pathogens
In heavy clay soil, add river sand (1:1 ratio) to improve drainage

title: Variety Selection
description: Choosing the right variety is critical for market success. Popular commercial varieties differ in fruit colour, taste, seed softness, and disease resistance.
tips: Bhagwa (Arakta): Deep red, soft seeds, high yield — most popular commercial variety
Wonderful: Dark red, tart-sweet flavour, suited for cooler climates
Mridula: Early bearing, deep red arils, good shelf life
Phule Bhagwa: Improved Bhagwa with higher disease tolerance
Sinduri: Suitable for drier regions, medium-sized fruits

title: Planting & Spacing
description: Correct spacing ensures adequate sunlight, air movement, and ease of intercultural operations. Pomegranate can be trained as a single-stemmed tree or multi-stemmed bush.
tips: Standard spacing: 4.5 m × 3.0 m (740 plants/hectare)
High-density spacing: 3 m × 2 m (1,666 plants/hectare) — requires pruning management
Best planting time: July–August (monsoon onset) or February–March
Plant grafted/ budded seedlings rather than seedlings for uniformity
Irrigate immediately after planting and keep soil moist for 2–3 weeks

title: Irrigation Management
description: Pomegranate is drought-tolerant once established but requires regular irrigation for commercial fruit production. Drip irrigation is highly recommended for water use efficiency.
tips: Install drip irrigation: 2 drip emitters per plant at 0.2–0.5 m from trunk
Young plants (0–2 years): irrigate every 3–4 days
Bearing trees: 4–6 litres/plant/day during dry season
Withhold irrigation for 2–3 months to induce flowering (stress method)
Resume irrigation after pruning to trigger bud break and flowering
Avoid water stress during fruit development — causes fruit cracking

title: Fertilisation Schedule
description: Balanced nutrition is key to high yield and fruit quality. Split fertiliser application reduces nutrient loss and improves uptake efficiency.
tips: Year 1: 10 kg FYM + 300:100:200 g N:P:K per plant per year
Bearing trees: 50 kg FYM + 625:375:250 g N:P:K per plant per year
Apply nitrogen in 3 splits: at pruning, fruit set, and fruit development
Micronutrient spray (Zinc sulphate 0.5% + Boric acid 0.1%) twice a year
Magnesium sulphate 0.5% foliar spray improves fruit colour and quality

title: Pruning & Training
description: Pruning regulates crop load, improves fruit quality, and controls plant size. For commercial orchards, single-stem or 3-stem training is recommended to maximise production.
tips: Train to single trunk or keep 3–4 primary branches
Remove suckers emerging from base regularly
Annual pruning after each crop harvest
Prune out dead, diseased, crossing, and weak branches
Deblossoming during first 2 years helps establish strong framework
Summer pruning (October–November in India) promotes concentrated flowering
